channel.title: My AI Filtered News
channel.description: Cleaned news feed via Gemini AI

item.title: At least 11 killed in Israeli air strikes across southern Lebanon
item.description: World Food Programe says situation in Lebnon is 'rapidly becoming a food security crisis' amid Israeli attacks.

item.title: Sorry Keir Starmer, the Middle East does not listen to Britain any more
item.description: Britain's PM visited the Gulf and said many of the right things. But when you do the wrong things, words don't matter.

item.title: Zelenskyy says Ukrainian interceptors downed Iran drones in the Middle East
item.description: Ukraine's Zelenskyy previously announced that 228 Ukrainian experts had been deployed to the region.

item.title: Inflation rises in US amid Iran war, Hormuz blockade
item.description: Government report shows gasoline going up by 21.2 percent in March as petrol remains above $4 per gallon despite truce.

item.title: ‘It’s really slapdash’: Trump administration coughs up to egregious errors to justify New York health fraud probe
item.description: The Trump administration admitted to the AP that it made significant errors in figures to justify a fraud probe into New York's Medicaid program.

item.title: Anthropic is limiting access to its latest AI model, Mythos. The real risks may already be out there
item.description: Anthropic is limiting access to its latest model, but similar capabilities may already be within reach.

item.title: Ukraine will have the most important defense industrial base in the free world, former CIA chief predicts
item.description: "It is producing cutting-edge unmanned systems, not just in the air, but on the ground and at sea."

item.title: ‘The Pitt’ star thought he’d ‘die’ with $80K in student debt—he was working 3 jobs, ready to ditch acting and move to the Alaskan wilderness
item.description: Patrick Ball, who plays senior resident Dr. Frank Langdon on “The Pitt,” admits he nearly quit acting altogether after struggling to make ends meet, even years after earning two degrees.

item.title: White-collar workers are quietly rebelling against AI as 80% outright refuse adoption mandates
item.description: "AI didn't deliver," Johns Hopkins professor Steve Hanke told Fortune. "Welcome to the real world. Forget the AI bubble."

item.title: United Airlines CEO judges candidates by whether pilots would want to go on a four-day trip with them—if not, they don’t get hired
item.description: Scott Kirby, the CEO of $31.7 billion company United Airlines, said he looks to hire employees who others would “want to hang out with.”

item.title: Argentina Tweaks FX Rules After Cost of Getting Dollars Out Surges
item.description: Argentina’s government issued new rules for investors moving money out of the country after increased use of the parallel foreign-exchange market drove the cost of such trades to its highest in a year.

item.title: Iran War Spurs States to Consider Gas Tax Halt as Fuel Costs Surge
item.description: The war in Iran is prompting states across the country to consider temporarily cutting fuel taxes, a relief measure that could cost them millions of dollars.

item.title: US Consumers 'Feeling Stretched' by High Prices, UMich's Hsu Says
item.description: University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers Director Joanne Hsu says US consumers are frustrated with the persistence of inflation but there is a glimmer of hope in the long-term outlook. She speaks on "Bloomberg Open Interest." (Source: Bloomberg)

item.title: Man arrested after baby killed in suspected dog attack in Yorkshire
item.description:
Woman also treated in hospital for arm injury as result of a dog bite

A 45-year-old man has been arrested after a three-month-old baby girl died in a suspected dog attack.

Cleveland police said they were called to a house in the Dormanstown area of Redcar shortly after 1.30pm on Thursday afternoon after a report of concern for the welfare of the child. The baby is believed to have died as a result of a dog bite, police said.

Continue reading...

item.title: Home Office starts crackdown on EU citizens’ post-Brexit rights to live in UK
item.description:
Concerns raised over use of travel data in determining whether people are ‘continuously’ in UK, after HMRC fiasco

Ministers are to start removing post-Brexit residency rights from EU citizens who are no longer “continuously” living in the UK.

The initiative is legal under the 2020 Brexit withdrawal agreement but the decision to use travel data to partially determine absences has raised concerns following the HMRC fiasco that saw almost 20,000 parents stripped of child benefits because of inaccurate Home Office border data.

Continue reading...

item.title: Labour MPs propose specialist sexual offences courts to help trials backlog
item.description:
Rebels put forward amendments to courts bill in attempt to stop government plans to cut back on jury trials

Labour MPs are hoping to hijack plans to cut back on jury trials in England and Wales by proposing specialist courts for sexual offences with fixed dates for trial.

While those behind the amendment want to block the wider plan to stop thousands of cases being potentially eligible for jury trials, a measure ministers insist is needed to cut court backlogs, they say the specialist courts alone could still solve much of the problem.

Continue reading...

item.title: Met police accused of favouring Tommy Robinson far-right rally over Palestine march
item.description:
Celebrities including Annie Lennox and Miriam Margolyes sign letter to force after pro-Palestine march route rejected

Annie Lennox and Miriam Margolyes are among artists who have accused the Metropolitan police of giving preferential treatment to a far-right demonstration led by Tommy Robinson over a pro-Palestine protest in London on the same day.

The pro-Palestine movement has had its preferred route through central London for its annual commemoration of Nakba – the mass expulsion of Palestinians – rejected by the Met, while the “Unite the Kingdom” demonstration will take place on the same date in Kingsway, the Strand, Trafalgar Square, Whitehall and Parliament Square. Robinson, whose real name is Stephen Yaxley-Lennon, posted on X: “London is ours on May 16th.”

Continue reading...

item.title: Fuel-price protests cause chaos in Ireland and spread to Norway
item.description:
Hauliers and farmers block motorways and bring parts of Dublin to a standstill in fourth day of action

  • Middle East crisis live – latest updates

Protests over fuel prices have caused chaos in Ireland and spread to Norway in a knock-on effect from the conflict in the Middle East.

Hauliers, farmers and other groups blocked motorways and brought parts of Dublin to a standstill on Friday in a fourth consecutive day of action.

Continue reading...

item.title: Orbán and rival Magyar hold rallies as Hungary election campaigns enter final stretch – Europe live
item.description:
Opposition candidate Péter Magyar holds rally outside Budapest as Orbán visits Székesfehérvár ahead of Sunday election

Ashifa Kassam and Flora Garamvolgyi in Budapest

As a child growing up in Budapest, Péter Magyar had a poster of Viktor Orbán – at the time a leading figure in the country’s pro-democracy movement – hanging above his bed.

Continue reading...

item.title: Labor’s planned gambling ad limits have influencer ‘blind spots’, experts warn
item.description:
‘Predatory industry’ adept at pivoting quickly to target new mediums and markets, says independent senator David Pocock

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Labor’s plan for a partial ban on gambling advertising is ripe for exploitation, say experts and crossbench MPs, including David Pocock, warning rules applying to podcasters, social media platforms and influencers need to be tightened.

But industry sources say podcast companies including Apple could consider removing all wagering advertising if there were no simple mechanism to work within the rules.

Continue reading...
